title: Urine (Fresh or Aged) on Cotton
stainChemistry: Urine contains urea (protein breakdown product), uric acid crystals (oxidizable), and urochrome pigment (yellow dye). Fresh urine is acidic (pH 4.5-6.5); its uric acid bonds weakly to cotton. Urochrome is a breakdown product of hemoglobin and stains yellow. Bacteria multiply over time, breaking down urea and producing ammonia—the characteristic odor. Cotton's cellulose is robust enough to tolerate enzymes, heat, and oxidizing bleach.
whyThisWorks: Urine is a biological stain — protein + uric acid + bacterial byproducts — so bio-enzymatic (enzymatic) treatment is the correct first move. The enzyme breaks urea and uric acid protein bonds. H₂O₂ oxidizes urochrome pigment to colorless. Cold water first prevents bacterial growth. Oxygen bleach (OxiClean) is thorough for old stains. White cotton tolerates aggressive treatment.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Work from the back of the garment first. Spray cold water and blot hard—this hydraulically flushes initial urine and uric acid crystals before bacteria establish. Flip to front and continue. Speed matters for fresh urine (within 1-2 hours). Keep blotting until you see only clear or pale yellow water. Cold water is critical; warm water activates bacteria and sets the odor.

spottingProtocol.agent: Protein solution (commercial enzyme powder + warm water)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Mix Protein per product instructions with warm water (30-40°C). White cotton tolerates warmth safely. Submerge stained area completely. Enzymes break peptide bonds in urea and uric acid molecules, reducing them to fragments. After 15 minutes, gently agitate or brush with a soft brush. You'll feel the stain texture change as protein fragments detach. Don't be aggressive; enzyme does the molecular work.

After Protein, apply 28% Acetic Acid (diluted) directly or soak in vinegar solution . Acetic acid dissolves any remaining uric acid crystals and helps neutralize bacterial odor. Vinegar also kills bacteria actively producing ammonia smell. This step is optional but highly recommended for urine because odor is the real problem—the stain itself is minor. Vinegar tackles both.

Flush thoroughly with NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) to remove all chemistry residue. Work from back of fabric. Blot — do not rub. All oxidizer and enzyme residue must be fully removed before drying.

spottingProtocol.agent: Baking soda solution or another vinegar pass (for odor)
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: If using paste: 10-15 minutes. If solution: soak 5-10 minutes
spottingProtocol.instruction: Urine odor is the real issue. After peroxide, apply baking soda paste (baking soda + water) directly to the stained area. Baking soda is alkaline and neutralizes any residual acidic urine compounds and ammonia. Let sit 10-15 minutes. Alternatively, do a final vinegar soak . Either approach kills remaining bacterial smell.

spottingProtocol.technique: Rinse thoroughly, then machine wash hot
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: Rinse until clear; wash on hot with detergent
spottingProtocol.instruction: Rinse under cold running water until all enzyme, vinegar, peroxide, and baking soda are gone. Any residual chemistry left behind will reactivate odor. Then machine wash on hot water (65-95°C) with regular detergent. Heat kills any remaining bacteria and sets the odor removal. Hot water is fine for white cotton. This hot wash is essential—it's the final bacteria kill.

spottingProtocol.agent: Heat dry or line dry in sunlight
spottingProtocol.instruction: Machine dry on high heat is fine for white cotton. Or hang in direct sunlight—UV light has antimicrobial effect and will further suppress any residual odor-causing bacteria. Heat won't re-set urine stains on cotton. Sunlight drying is actually preferred for urine because UV kills bacteria. Bring inside once dry.

homeSolutions.instruction: Test on hidden area first to ensure fabric tolerates the solutions.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never use hot water on urine — heat sets protein stains.

homeSolutions.instruction: Place fabric stain-side down on glass work surface. Dab the stain lightly with cool water applied to a white cloth.
Apply detergent/ammonia solution (1/4 tbsp mild laundry detergent + 1/2 tsp ammonia + 4 oz cool water) to the stain. Rub gently with the back of a spoon.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Ammonia can be strong — ensure good ventilation.

homeSolutions.instruction: Rinse thoroughly with cool water, working from center outward.
Apply hydrogen peroxide 3% mixed with ammonia (equal parts) and allow to sit for 3 minutes to oxidize protein residue.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Test on hidden area first — peroxide may lighten some dyes.

homeSolutions.instruction: Apply white vinegar to neutralize and remove any rings. Rinse again with cool water. Wipe rings outward from center.
If urine stain persists or if fabric is delicate, call a professional cleaner.

materialWarnings: DO NOT use hot water initially—activates bacteria and sets odor
DO NOT skip the hot wash—cold water won't kill bacteria
AVOID mixing vinegar and bleach—creates toxic chlorine gas
escalation: If odor persists after home treatment, the stain has bacteria deeply embedded. Professional cleaners use commercial enzymatic systems (like Protenzyme or Chemspec enzyme products at high concentration) combined with antimicrobial rinses. For mattresses or upholstery, professional enzymatic treatment is the only reliable option.

household.name: 3% H₂O₂
household.note: Oxidizes urochrome pigment to colorless. Fast (5 min). Safe for white cotton.

household.name: White Vinegar
household.note: Dissolves uric acid, kills odor-causing bacteria. Essential for urine stains. DO NOT substitute industrial-strength (28%) acetic acid — it causes chemical burns.

household.name: Baking Soda
household.note: Neutralizes ammonia and acidic urine compounds. Apply as paste for odor removal.

household.name: OxiClean
household.note: Sodium percarbonate. Gentler than peroxide, works 30-60 minutes. Safe for white.

professional.name: Protein (concentrated)
professional.note: Professional-strength enzyme. Faster breakdown of urine proteins than household.

professional.name: StreePene (Sodium Hydrosulfite)
professional.note: For oxidized/aged urochrome. Professional shops use for old urine stains.

customerExplanation: Urine is urea protein, uric acid crystals, and urochrome pigment. Bacteria multiply and break down urea, creating ammonia odor. Enzyme breaks the protein, vinegar dissolves crystals and kills bacteria, peroxide removes color. White cotton tolerates all of this chemistry. The real challenge is odor—bacteria need to be fully killed.
commonMistakes: Using warm water first—this accelerates bacterial growth and sets odor permanently
Skipping vinegar—uric acid remains and bacteria smell continues
Not doing a hot wash—bacteria need heat to be killed. Cold wash won't work.
Not air drying in sunlight—UV light kills remaining odor bacteria. Skip this and odor lingers.
scienceNote: Uric acid (C5H4N4O3) is a purine metabolite. Fresh urine is acidic and uric acid stays ionized and relatively mobile. As bacteria multiply, they break down urea (NH2-CO-NH2) into ammonia (NH3), raising pH above 7. Alkaline urine precipitates uric acid as crystals and promotes ammonia production—this is why old urine smells. Enzyme breaks down the urea substrate itself, cutting off the bacteria's food source.
callPro: For stains aged weeks or months, or for urine on mattresses/upholstery where bacteria is embedded in padding, professional enzymatic treatment is essential. They use high-concentration enzyme systems and antimicrobial rinses that home chemistry can't match. Odor in upholstery requires professional equipment to force enzymes deep into the padding.
solventNote: Professional: High-concentration Protein (2-5%) in warm water (40°C) for 20-30 minutes, vinegar rinse (1:1 with water) for bacterial kill, then StreePene solution if stain is aged/brown, then hot wash. For embedded odor in padding, use injection extraction—force enzyme solution into padding, let dwell 20 minutes, extract with hot water. Follow with antimicrobial rinse.
